description: An iconic symbol and national treasure of Cambodia, the sugar palm tree has been beloved by people of this country for generations. Almost every part of it can be used. Fun fact: there is a famous Khmer saying about sugar palm trees that goes “Drop by drop, the bottle is filled”, referring to the slow drop of sweet palm sap that fills the pipe used to collect it, a process that may take half a day and requires immense patience to complete.

A favorite cultural snack eaten seasonally at the end of the dry season, the Java Plum is a fruit found on trees all across Cambodia. During the period of Khmer New Year, it is commonly eaten as a snack with dipping salts composed of salt, sugar, MSG, and chili peppers. Fun fact: compounds in the fruit can stain your tongue purple and blue.

With blossoms often mistaken for those of strawberry plants, the Jamaican Cherry tree has long been another popular snack of Cambodians. The trees are resilient and can often be found by roadsides, with blooms and fruit all year round. The fruit is sweet, comparable to the taste of cotton candy or caramel, with several thousands of tiny yellow seeds inside. Fun fact: Jamaican cherries have no relation to common cherries!

khmer: ចាន់គិរី​/ចាន់រុី
description: A common source of shade from the grueling sun, the Monkey Pod Tree is a more rurally-found tree in Cambodia. A massive tree commonly used as a local gathering place, it can reach up to heights of 15-25 meters, with canopy diameters of up to 61 meters. Timber and shade have been the most common uses for this tree. Fun fact: it directs water to its roots by folding up its leaves at night, allowing rainwater to fall straight down.
